blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَعْطَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْكَوْثَرَ (۱)
Indeed We have given you abundance. (1)
فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَٱنْحَرْ (۲)
So pray to your Lord, and sacrifice [the sacrificial camel]. (2)
إِنَّ شَانِئَكَ هُوَ ٱلْأَبْتَرُ (۳)
Indeed it is your enemy who is without posterity. (3)

Key Points

When Allah is inviting to Tawhid (Monotheism), He uses the singular pronoun, such as: «أنا ربّكم فاعبدون» (185) I am your Lord, so worship Me. «أنَا ربّكم فاتَّقون» (186) I am your Lord, so fear Me. «انّنى أنَا اللّه» (187) Indeed, I am Allah, the One God. Similarly, the singular pronoun is used when Allah performs an action without an intermediary, or when He is expressing the close connection between the Creator and the creation, such as: «و أنَا الغفور الرحیم» (188) And I alone am the Forgiving, the Merciful. «فانّى قریب» (189) Indeed, I am near to My servants. However, sometimes the Ayah is meant to express the Greatness of Allah and the honor of the blessing, in which case the plural pronoun, such as «اِنّا» (We), is used. As He says in this Ayah: «اِنّا اعطیناك الكوثر» (Indeed, We have granted you Al-Kawthar), because the Divine gift to the best of Allah's creation, and that gift being Al-Kawthar, must be mentioned with عظمت (greatness). Furthermore, when Allah performs an action through intermediaries, such as rain which falls through the intermediary of the sun's radiation, the creation of vapor, and clouds, the plural pronoun is used, as He says: «وانزلنا من السماء ماءً» (190) And We sent down water from the sky.

Out of the one hundred and fourteen Surahs of the Quran, four Surahs begin with the word «اِنّا» (Indeed, We): Surahs Al-Fath, Nuh, Al-Qadr, and Al-Kawthar. «اِنّا فَتَحنا لك فتحاً مبیناً» Indeed, We have granted you a clear victory. «اِنّا ارسلنا نوحاً» Indeed, We sent Nuh. «اِنّا انزلناه فى لیلة القدر» Indeed, We revealed it on the Night of Qadr. «اِنّا اعطیناك الكوثر» Indeed, We have granted you Al-Kawthar.

At the beginning of one of these four Surahs, there is mention of Prophet Nuh, who is the first of the Ulul-'Azm Prophets and is considered the second father of humanity after Prophet Adam. At the beginning of the other three Surahs, special blessings are mentioned, such as the revelation of the Quran, a clear victory, and the granting of Al-Kawthar. Perhaps there is a connection between these four subjects: one expresses the mission of the first Ulul-'Azm Prophet, the second the revelation of the last heavenly book, the third the victory of the school of Islam, and the fourth the continuation of the line of prophethood and its preservation from being abtar (cut off).

The word «كوثر» (Kawthar) is derived from kathrat (abundance) and means abundant and copious good (khayr kathir). It is clear that this meaning can have multiple manifestations, such as revelation, prophethood, the Quran, the status of intercession, vast knowledge, and good morals. However, the last Ayah of the Surah can serve as evidence that the intent of Kawthar is the blessed progeny of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). This is because the hateful enemy audaciously insulted the Prophet by calling him abtar (cut off), and Allah, in defense of His Prophet, said: «انّ شانئك هو الابتر» Indeed, your enemy is the one cut off. Therefore, if the intent of Kawthar were not progeny, we would not have a pleasing justification or meaning for the connection between the first and third Ayahs of the Surah.

The word «ابتر» (Abtar) originally refers to an animal with a cut-off tail, and idiomatically, it refers to someone who leaves no lasting progeny. Since the Prophet's sons passed away in childhood, the enemies used to say: He has no successor or progeny and is abtar. This was because, in the culture of Jahiliyya (Ignorance), a daughter was not considered worthy of keeping her father's name alive. Therefore, the phrase «انّ شانئك هو الابتر» is evidence that the intent of Kawthar is the Prophet's numerous progeny, which undoubtedly came through Lady Fatimah (عليها السلام). This blessed progeny was granted to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) through Lady Khadijah. Yes, Khadijah gave abundant wealth and received Kawthar. We, too, will not reach Kawthar unless we sacrifice the abundant.

Fakhr al-Razi says in his Tafsir Kabir: What progeny is more blessed than the progeny of Fatimah, from which figures like Baqir, Sadiq, and Rida arose? And although a great number of them were martyred throughout history, especially during the rule of the Umayyads and Abbasids, their descendants are still widespread in most Islamic countries today.

At a time when the news of a daughter's birth caused sorrow to the father, to the extent that his face darkened with grief and he contemplated whether to hide from the people or bury his daughter alive: «یتوارى من القوم ام یدُسّه فى التّراب» (191), at this time, the Quran gives the daughter the title Kawthar, in order to transform the culture of Jahiliyya into a Divine and humane culture.

Based on narrations, the name of one of the rivers or fountains in Paradise is Kawthar, and the believers will be quenched by it. Just as in the continuously transmitted Hadith of Thaqalayn, the Prophet said: «انّهما لن یفترقا حتّى یردا علىّ الحوض» The Book and the Progeny will not separate until they meet Me at the Fountain on the Day of Judgment.

Divine Kawthar is not dependent on gender; Fatimah was a daughter, yet she became Kawthar. It is not dependent on large numbers; Fatimah was one person, yet she became Kawthar. Yes, Allah can make the little abundant (Kawthar) and erase the abundant.

Allah defends His chosen ones. He gives both a verbal and a practical response to the one who considered the Prophet to be without succession. The existence of Fatimah is the practical response, and the phrase «هو الابتر» is the verbal response. Allah defends not only His chosen ones but all believers: «انّ اللّه یدافع عن الّذین آمنوا» (192)

The Giver is Allah, the receiver is the Prophet, and the gift is Fatimah. Hence, the phrase «اِنّا اعطیناك الكوثر» is used, which expresses the Greatness of Allah and the magnitude of His gift.

The more severe the attack on religion and religious sanctities, the stronger the defense must be. Many insults were directed at the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله); he was called a sorcerer, soothsayer, poet, and madman, all of which were compensated for in some way in the Quran. They said to the Prophet: «انّك لمجنون» (193) You are possessed, but Allah said: «ما انتَ بنعمة ربّك بمجنون» (194) By the grace of your Lord, you are not possessed. They said to him: «لَستَ مُرسلاً» (195) You are not a messenger of God. Allah said: «انّك لمن المرسلین» (196) Indeed, you are surely among the messengers. They attributed poetry and fantasy to him: «لشاعر مجنون» (197) Allah said: «و ما علّمناه الشعر و ما ینبغى له» (198) We did not teach him poetry, nor is it appropriate for him. They said: What kind of messenger is this who eats food and walks in the markets? «و قالوا ما لهذا الرسول یاكل الطعام و یمشى فى الاسواق» (199) Allah said: The messengers before you also ate food and walked in the markets: «و ما ارسلنا قبلك من المرسلین الاّ انّهم لیاكلون الطعام و یمشون فى الاسواق» (200) They called him abtar, and Allah said: «انّ شانئك هو الابتر». Yes, the response to someone who calls the Noblest of Creation abtar is that Allah grants Him a Kawthar that dazzles all eyes and astounds all minds.

Allah did not specify a manifestation for Kawthar in the Ayah, so that it remains ambiguous, perhaps indicating that the depth of the blessing of the granted Kawthar is veiled in ambiguity even for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) Himself.

Two hidden prophecies are contained in this Surah: one is the granting of Kawthar to the Prophet, especially in Mecca where he was empty-handed and had no son; the other is the failure (abtar status) of the enemy, who possessed many sons and great wealth.

History and statistics are the best witnesses to the Kawthar nature of this Divine gift. No progeny of any nation in the world has grown and flourished as much as the progeny of Lady Fatimah (عليها السلام). Especially if those whose mothers are Sayyid are counted among the Sayyids, the statistics of Sayyids in the world are a sign of the miracle of this hidden prophecy.

Granting Kawthar to a person like the Prophet only makes sense if the Giver is the source of knowledge, wisdom, power, and mercy. Therefore, the granting of Kawthar is a sign of Divine attributes and perfections.

In this Surah, which has only three sentences, the Prophet Himself is addressed five times. In addition to the pronoun kaf (ك) in «اعطیناك», the Prophet is addressed twice in the command «صلّ» and the phrase «لربك», and twice in the phrases «انحر» and «شانئك». «اعطیناك، فصلّ، لربّك، انحر، شانئك»

Throughout the entire Quran, Allah addresses His Prophet more than two hundred times with the term «ربّك» (Your Lord), even though He is the Lord of all existence: «ربّ كلّ شى ء» (201) and the Lord of all people: «ربّ النّاس» (202). But among the terms for "Lord," the word «ربّك» is used most frequently, which indicates that Allah has a special favor for His Prophet. We see examples of this special favor in other Ayahs as well. For instance, Allah has mentioned the names of the Prophet's limbs and organs in the Quran: Your face: «وجهك» (203), Your tongue: «لسانك» (204), Your eyes: «عینیك» (205), Your neck: «عنقك» (206), Your hands: «یدك» (207), Your chest: «صدرك» (208), Your back: «ظهرك» (209).

The favors of Allah upon the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) sometimes occurred upon His request, such as: «قل ربّ زدنى علماً» (210) Say: My Lord! Increase me in knowledge. But Kawthar was a Divine gift given to the dear Prophet without request.

What is Kawthar? It is understood from the last Ayah of the Surah, namely «انّ شانئك هو الابتر», that Kawthar is something opposite to abtar. Since the Arabs call individuals who are without succession or progeny, who have no sons, and whose traces will be erased upon death, abtar, the best manifestation for Kawthar is the progeny of the Prophet, meaning the infallible Imams from the line of Fatimah. Of course, Kawthar has a general meaning and includes all abundant good (khayr kathir).

If Kawthar means knowledge, it is the very thing the Prophet was commanded to ask for: «قل ربّ زدنى علماً». If Kawthar means good morals, the Prophet possessed sublime character: «انّك لعلى خلق عظیم» (211). If Kawthar means worship, the Prophet worshipped so much that an Ayah was revealed: We did not send down the Quran to you that you should distress yourself in this way: «ما انزلنا علیك القرآن لتشقى» (212). If Kawthar means numerous progeny, then today the largest progeny belongs to Him. If Kawthar means a large nation, then according to the Divine promise, Islam will prevail over the entire world: «لیظهره على الدین كلّه» (213). If Kawthar means intercession, Allah will forgive His nation to the extent that the Prophet is satisfied: «و لسوف یعطیك ربّك فترضى» (214).

Not every abundance is Kawthar. The Quran says: Do not let the wealth and children of the opponents impress you, for Allah only intends to punish them through these things in the life of this world: «فلا تعجبك اموالهم و لا اولادهم انما یرید اللّه لیعذّبهم بها فى الحیاة الدنیا» (215).

In the Quran, we have a Surah named Kawthar and a Surah named Takathur. But Kawthar is a value, and Takathur is an anti-value, because the first is a Divine gift followed by the remembrance of God: «اعطیناك الكوثر فصلّ», and the second is a negative competition followed by heedlessness of God: «الهاكم التكاثر». Kawthar leads us to the mosque for prayer: «فصلّ لربّك», and Takathur leads us to the graveyard for counting the dead: «حتى زُرتم المقابر». In the granting of Kawthar there is good news: «انّا اعطیناك الكوثر», and in Takathur there are successive threats: «كلاّ سوف تعلمون ثمّ كلاّ سوف تعلمون». Kawthar is a factor in the relationship with the Creator: «اعطیناك الكوثر فصل», and Takathur is a means of preoccupation with creation: «الهاكم التّكاثر».

Kawthar, which is the greatest Divine gift, is presented in the smallest Surah of the Quran. The gift of the Noblest Worshipped to the Noblest of Creation can be nothing but Kawthar. «انّا اعطیناك الكوثر»

In the event of the Conquest of Mecca, when polytheists were entering Islam in groups and droves, Allah only commanded glorification: «رَأیتَ النَّاس یَدخلون فى دینِ اللَّه أفواجا فَسَبّح». But for the granting of Kawthar, He says: «فصلِ ّ لربّك» Perform prayer. It is as if the importance of Kawthar is greater than the conversion of the polytheists to Islam.

An exceptional gift in an exceptional Surah with exceptional words: The gift is unique because it is Kawthar; the Surah is unique because it is the smallest Surah of the Quran; and the words are unique because the terms «اعطینا», «الكوثر», «صلِ ّ», «انحر», «شانئك», and «ابتر» are used only in this Surah and have no parallel anywhere else in the Quran.

Every verbal wound and word has weight. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) Himself was subjected to many insults, being called words like madman, poet, soothsayer, and sorcerer. His companions were also insulted, to the extent that they said: Drive these poor people away so that we may gather around you. For none of these was an independent Surah revealed. However, regarding the accusation of being abtar, a Surah was revealed stating that We have granted you abundant good, and your enemy is the one cut off. This is because insults to the person and companions are tolerable, but insults to the path and the school of thought—suggesting that His path is transient and without a future—are intolerable.

Sometimes the insult arises from idle talk, which should be passed by with dignity: «اذا مرّوا باللغو مرّوا كراما». Sometimes the insult is due to a corrupt environment and bad friends, from whom one must turn away: «اذا رأیت الّذین یخوضون فى آیاتنا فاعرض عنهم» (216). But sometimes the insult is directed at the school of thought and the leader, especially by prominent and political figures, which must be answered harshly: «انّ شانئك هو الابتر». Just as the hypocrites arrogantly said: Should we believe as the foolish have believed? «أنُومن كما آمن السفهاء» The Quran responds to the audacity of such people thus: «الا انّهم هم السّفهاء و لكن لا یعلمون» (217) Indeed, they are the fools, but they do not know!

The camel is mentioned in the Quran in the discussion of Tawhid: «أفلا ینظرون الى الابل كیف خلقت» (218), and in relation to the preliminaries of the Day of Judgment: «و اذا العشار عطّلت» (219), and in the rulings and rituals of Hajj: «و البُدن جعلناها» (220), and also in the context of sacrifice and aid to society: «وانحر».

Based on narrations, the intent of «وانحر» is to raise the hands during the takbirs (exclamations of Allahu Akbar) in prayer up to the hollow beneath the throat, which is the place of nahr (slaughter), and this is the adornment of prayer (221).

Based on narrations, the hateful enemy who insulted the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and called him abtar was the father of Amr ibn al-'As.

Allah is both the Creator of means and the Destroyer of means. He can create Kawthar from one Fatimah, and He can cause individuals who have strong sons to be forgotten. He can dry the sea by striking it with the staff of Moses: «اِضرب بعصاك البحر فانفلق» (222), and cause twelve springs to gush forth by striking a rock with the same staff: «و اضرب بعصاك الحجر فانفجرت» (223).

185) Al-Anbiya, 92. 186) Al-Mu'minun, 52. 187) Taha, 14. 188) Al-Hijr, 49. 189) Al-Baqarah, 186. 190) Al-Mu'minun, 18. 191) An-Nahl, 59. 192) Al-Hajj, 38. 193) Al-Hijr, 6. 194) Al-Qalam, 2. 195) Ar-Ra'd, 43. 196) Ya Sin, 3. 197) As-Saffat, 36. 198) Ya Sin, 69. 199) Al-Furqan, 7. 200) Al-Furqan, 20. 201) Al-An'am, 164. 202) An-Nas, 1. 203) Al-Baqarah, 144. 204) Al-Qiyamah, 16. 205) Al-Hijr, 88. 206) Al-Isra, 29. 207) Al-Isra, 29. 208) Al-A'raf, 2. 209) Ad-Duha, 3. 210) Taha, 114. 211) Al-Qalam, 4. 212) Taha, 2. 213) At-Tawbah, 33. 214) Ad-Duha, 5. 215) At-Tawbah, 55. 216) Al-An'am, 68. 217) Al-Baqarah, 13. 218) Al-Ghashiyah, 17. 219) At-Takwir, 4. 220) Al-Hajj, 36. 221) Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan. 222) Ash-Shu'ara, 63. 223) Al-Baqarah, 60.

Messages

1- Allah fulfills His promises. In Surah Ad-Duha, Allah had promised a gift to the Prophet: «و لسوف یعطیك ربّك فترضى» (224) And your Lord is going to give you, and you will be satisfied. In this Surah, He says: We have fulfilled that promise. «انّا اعطیناك الكوثر»

2- Children and progeny are a Divine gift. «انّا اعطیناك الكوثر»

3- Blessings, even for the Prophet of Islam, bring responsibility. «اعطیناك... فَصلِّ»

4- The Quran recommends prayer or prostration of gratitude. «فَصلِّ»

5- Gratitude must be immediate. «فَصلِّ» (The letter fa indicates immediacy.)

6- We must learn the method of gratitude from God. «فَصلِّ»

7- Let us not forget Allah during blessings and joys. «اعطیناك الكوثر فصلّ»

8- That which can serve as gratitude for Kawthar is prayer. «فَصلِّ» (Prayer is the most comprehensive and complete form of worship, in which the heart must be present with the intention of seeking closeness [to Allah], the tongue with the recitation of Al-Hamd and the Surah, and the body with bowing and prostration. The wiping of the head and feet may also indicate that man is His servant from head to toe. In prayer, the highest point of the body, the forehead, is rubbed on the ground thirty-four times a day so that no arrogance remains in the person. Lady Fatimah (عليها السلام) said in her famous sermon: The philosophy and reason for prayer is the purification of the soul from arrogance. «تنزیهاً لكم من الكبر»)

9- Religious commands are in accordance with reason and nature. Reason deems gratitude for blessings necessary, and religion commands the same. «فصلّ لربّك»

10- Since the gift is from Him: «انّا اعطینا», the gratitude must also be for Him. «فصلّ لربّك»

11- Sacrifice is one of the ways to express gratitude for Divine blessings. (Because the deprived benefit from it.) «و انحر»

12- The greater the roof, the more snow it catches. For one who has Kawthar, sacrificing a sheep is not enough; he must slaughter a camel (nahr) and sacrifice the largest domestic animal. «و انحر»

13- The relationship with the Creator precedes the relationship with creation. «فصلّ... و انحر»

14- Charity (infaq) is valuable when it is accompanied by faith and worship. «فصلّ لربّك وانحر»

15- The gratitude for receiving a gift from God is giving to the people. «اعطیناك الكوثر... وانحر»

16- Prayer is valuable when it is sincere, «لربّك», and charity is valuable when it is generous. «وانحر»

17- The enemies of the Prophet and his school of thought are unsuccessful. (The word shani' is an active participle and includes every enemy in the past, present, and future. If it had said: «من شانك هو الابتر», it would only include those who showed enmity in the past, and if it had said: «من یشونك هو الابتر», it would only include future enemies. «انّ شانئك هو الابتر».)

18- Insulting sanctities incurs severe rebuke and threat. «انّ شانئك هو الابتر»

19- Do not fear taunts and insults, for Allah protects His supporters. «انّ شانئك هو الابتر»

20- Do not judge hastily and do not rely solely on statistics and calculations, for everything depends on the Will of Allah. (The opponents judged that the Prophet was abtar due to the death of his son and their own possession of multiple sons, but everything turned out the opposite.) «انّا اعطیناك الكوثر... انّ شانئك هو الابتر»

224) Ad-Duha, 5.

Explanations

Portrait of Surah Al-Kawthar

This Surah has three Ayahs and was revealed in Mecca, and it is the smallest Surah of the Holy Quran. The name of the Surah is taken from the first Ayah and means abundant and copious good (khayr kathir). Based on numerous narrations from both Shia and Sunni sources, 'As ibn Wa'il, the father of Amr ibn al-'As, who was one of the prominent polytheists of Mecca, taunted and insulted the Prophet by calling him abtar (cut off and without succession) after the Prophet's sons passed away and he had no other son. Allah revealed Surah Al-Kawthar to console the Prophet and respond to this inappropriate remark, announcing the failure of the enemies and the permanence and survival of the Prophet's progeny and thought. In narrations, whoever recites this Surah in obligatory and recommended prayers is promised to be quenched from the Fountain of Kawthar on the Day of Judgment (184).

184) Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn.
